text: The endoscope is inserted to perform an initial assessment of the near-circumferential duodenal lesion involving the major papilla, providing a clear view of the lesion's extent and characteristics.
The endoscopic view reveals a near-circumferential duodenal laterally spreading lesion involving the major papilla.
The surgeon observes the laterally spreading component of the duodenal lesion, which extends approximately six centimeters in the longitudinal direction.
The surgeon uses a Carlock needle in conjunction with the duodenoscope, visible within the duodenal lumen during the ampullectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a sequential submucosal injection beneath the laterally spreading lesion using a solution containing succinylated gelatin, indigo carmine, and adrenaline diluted to one in a hundred thousand.
The surgeon uses a 15mm oval snare with a 0.47mm thickness wire to perform sequential mucosal resection of the duodenal lesion, carefully excising the tissue in sections.
The surgeon removes the laterally spreading lesion (LSL) component, which is visually identifiable near the major papilla in the duodenum.
The surgeon uses standard EMR polypectomy settings on the ERBI electro generator, with endocut Q effect set to three, cut duration to one, and cut interval to six, to perform the resection.
The surgeon uses the edge of a previous resection defect as an anchor for the snare, allowing for controlled capture of the surrounding tissue during the ampullectomy procedure.
The surgeon assesses the mobility of the captured tissue relative to the adjacent tissue before proceeding with electrocautery resection.
The surgeon confirms the absence of muscularis propria involvement, ensuring that the resection avoids deep mural injury.
The surgeon performs sequential endoscopic mucosal resection of the laterally spreading lesion, using a snare to capture and resect the targeted tissue under endoscopic visualization.
Following a resection, visible bleeding occurs at the site, requiring careful management to control the risk of hemorrhage.
The surgeon irrigates the bleeding area using a triple lumen synchrotome, revealing a transected arteriole within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a coagulation grasper to address the bleeding arteriole, which is too large for sneer tip soft coagulation.
The surgeon captures the vessel using a coagulation grasper, resulting in the cessation of bleeding from the transected arteriole.
The surgeon uses forceps to tent the vessel away from the mucosa and applies a soft coagulating current at eighty watts to obliterate the vessel.
The surgeon completes the sequential endoscopic mucosal resection of the laterally spreading component, leaving the ampullary adenoma visible in the surgical field.
The ampullary adenoma is captured en bloc with a snare, which is fully tightened to perform transection using the endoscopic mucosal resection settings.
A five French single pigtail plastic pancreatic stent is visually placed into the pancreatic duct as a prophylactic measure.
The surgeon places a fully covered metal biliary stent, measuring ten millimeters by six centimeters, into the biliary duct as a prophylactic measure against microperforation and bleeding.
